meta.title: Hurricanes
meta.author: Shaun Taylor
meta.focusQuestion: How do hurricanes form, and how do we prepare for them?

sections.title: A Hurricane Is Coming!
paragraphs: "This just in from the National Hurricane Center in Miami. <s>A hurricane watch</s> <v>is</v> <o>in effect</o> for the Florida east coast from Florida City north to Flagler Beach, including Lake Okeechobee. <s>Hurricane Frances</s> <v>is</v> <o>about 555 miles east-southeast of Palm Beach</o> and <v>is moving</v> toward the west-northwest at about 14 miles per hour."

<s>The message</s> <v>crackles</v> <o>over a small Florida cafe radio</o>. <s>The patrons</s> <v>look</v> outside at a warm, sunny day and then out to sea for any signs of the approaching storm—there are none. However, <s>the television</s> <v>shows</v> <o>a satellite image of a gigantic, spiraling storm</o>. <s>A dotted line</s> <v>shows</v> <o>the hurricane's path</o> and <s>a shaded wedge</s> <v>shows</v> <o>where the hurricane might hit the coast</o>.

<s>The people in the cafe</s> have already been hit with one hurricane this season, and <s>they</s> <v>know</v> <o>they must prepare</o>. <s>Some</s> <v>buy</v> <o>more plywood to board up windows</o>. <s>Others</s> <v>get</v> <o>supplies at the grocery store</o>, such as water, food, and batteries.

<s>The next morning</s>, <s>the wind speed</s> <v>picks up</v>, and <s>the sky</s> <v>clouds over</v>. <s>Some people farther down the coast</s> <v>evacuate</v>, or <v>leave</v> <o>their homes to go to safer areas</o>. <s>Winds</s> <v>reach</v> <o>hurricane strength</o>, bending over palm trees as if they are made of rubber. <s>The wind</s> <v>rips</v> <o>roofs off buildings</o>, and <s>flying objects</s> <v>become</v> <o>deadly</o>. <s>High waves and rising water</s> <v>flood</v> <o>low-lying areas on the coast</o>.

<s>Falling trees</s> <v>break</v> <o>power lines</o>. <s>Some residents who were not evacuated</s> <v>sit</v> <o>in the darkness of their homes</o> listening to the storm's progress on their battery-powered radios. Finally, <s>the storm</s> <v>moves</v> inland, and <s>the wind</s> <v>slows down</v>; but <s>drenching rains and flooding</s> <v>remain</v>. <s>The next day</s> <s>the sun</s> <v>comes out</v>, and <s>people</s> <v>begin</v> <o>the costly cleanup</o>.

sections.title: What Is a Hurricane?
paragraphs: We have all experienced wind and rainstorms, but what makes a hurricane different? <s>Hurricanes</s> <v>are</v> <o>gigantic, circular storms that can be hundreds of kilometers wide</o>. When they occur in the Pacific Ocean, <s>they</s> <v>are called</v> <o>typhoons</o>. In the Indian Ocean, <s>they</s> <v>are called</v> <o>severe tropical cyclones</o>. In the Atlantic Ocean, <s>they</s> <v>are called</v> <o>hurricanes</o>. No matter what they are called, winds over hundreds of kilometers per hour, inches of rain, and high seas cause death, and sometimes billions of dollars in damage.

<s>Hurricanes</s> <v>have</v> <o>a distinct structure that can be seen on satellite photos</o>. <s>Clouds</s> <v>extend out</v> as <o>spiraling arms for hundreds of kilometers around the center</o>. <s>The center of a hurricane</s> <v>is called</v> <o>the eye</o>. <s>Winds</s> <v>become</v> more and more <o>intense</o> from the edge of the storm to the eyewall.

<s>The eyewall</s> <v>is</v> <o>where winds rushing toward the center suddenly turn upward</o>. <s>The upward-moving air</s> <v>cools</v> rapidly, and <s>rain</s> <v>begins to fall</v>. <s>Most of the rainfall</s> <v>occurs</v> <o>in the eyewall</o>. Sometimes rainfall reaches 20 millimeters (0.78 in.) of rain per hour, which can dump about 15–30 centimeters (6–12 in.) on one area over several days.

Yet, inside the eye, <s>it</s> <v>is</v> often <o>calm and sunny</o>. <s>Most hurricane eyes</s> <v>are</v> <o>30–60 kilometers (20–40 mi.) in diameter</o>. In the eye of a hurricane, <s>the peacefulness</s> <v>will not last long</v>. Soon <s>the eye</s> <v>will move</v>, and then <s>the sudden force of the other side of the storm</s> <v>will hit</v>.

As hurricanes approach shore, <s>the combination of high winds and low air pressure</s> actually <v>raises</v> <o>the level of the sea around the storm higher than normal</o>. <s>Scientists</s> <v>call</v> <o>this a storm surge</o>. <s>It</s> <v>extends</v> for <o>miles around the center of a hurricane</o> and <v>floods</v> <o>any low, coastal land</o>. <s>Flooding caused by a storm surge</s> <v>is</v> <o>a major source of property damage from hurricanes</o>.

sections.title: How Hurricanes Form
paragraphs: <s>Hurricanes</s> <v>form</v> <o>over tropical oceans worldwide</o>. These tropical storms tend to be seasonal. <s>They</s> usually <v>form</v> <o>in the summers of the Northern and Southern Hemispheres</o>.

<s>The storms</s> <v>form</v> when <s>energy from the Sun</s> <v>heats</v> <o>ocean water near the equator</o>. <s>Warm, moist air</s> <v>evaporates</v> from <o>the ocean</o> and <v>is</v> lighter than <o>the cold, dry air above it</o>. <s>The warmer air</s> <v>pushes up</v> through <o>the cooler air</o>, which then <v>sinks down</v> to take the place of the warmer air. <s>The process</s> <v>creates</v> <o>a low-pressure area called a tropical depression</o>.

As <s>the warm, moist air</s> <v>rises</v>, <s>it</s> <v>cools</v> and <v>condenses</v>, a process that <v>creates</v> <o>clouds</o> and <v>releases</v> <o>heat energy</o>. Soon <s>giant thunderclouds</s> <v>form</v>.

<s>Wind</s> <v>begins to rotate</v>, or spin, around <o>the low-pressure area</o>. <s>The wind</s> <v>starts blowing</v> harder, and when it reaches about 62 kilometers per hour (39 mph), <s>the tropical depression</s> <v>becomes</v> <o>a tropical storm</o>.

<s>Tropical storms</s> <v>can grow</v> into <o>hurricanes</o> if <s>they</s> <v>soak up</v> <o>more heat energy from the warm oceans they pass over</o>. Some storms don't ever gain enough energy to become hurricanes.

If <s>a tropical storm</s> <v>does gain</v> <o>more energy</o>, <s>wind speed</s> <v>picks up</v> and <s>thick spiral bands of clouds</s> <v>spread out</v> from <o>the center of the storm</o>. When wind speeds are greater than 119 kilometers per hour (74 mph), <s>the storm</s> officially <v>becomes</v> <o>a hurricane</o>.

Generally, <s>hurricanes in the Northern Hemisphere</s> <v>move</v> west, then north, and then <v>turn</v> toward the northeast. This path takes hurricanes away from the equator and away from the warm ocean water that fuels them. In the Southern Hemisphere <s>hurricanes</s> typically <v>travel</v> west, south, and then <v>turn</v> southeast, which also takes them away from warm ocean water.

As <s>hurricanes</s> <v>move</v> over <o>cooler water and over land</o>, <s>they</s> <v>lose</v> <o>much of their energy</o>. <s>The wind</s> <v>slows down</v>, and <s>the storms</s> eventually <v>disperse</v>.

sections.title: Where Hurricanes Form
paragraphs: <s>Central America, islands in the Caribbean, and the East Coast of the United States</s> <v>are hit</v> <o>by hurricanes almost every year</o>. Rarely <v>do</v> <s>hurricanes</s> <v>hit</v> <o>the U.S. West Coast</o>. <s>Atlantic Ocean hurricanes</s> <v>get started</v> <o>southeast of the Caribbean Sea</o>.

In the Pacific Ocean <s>typhoons</s> most often <v>hit</v> <o>Asia and Indonesia</o>. In the Indian Ocean, <s>severe tropical cyclones</s> <v>affect</v> <o>Australia, the Middle East, parts of Asia, and the eastern coast of Africa</o>.

During hurricane season (which can last six months or more), <s>meteorologists</s> <v>keep</v> <o>a careful watch on tropical depressions and tropical storms hundreds of kilometers offshore</o> to see if any will become hurricanes.

sections.title: How Hurricanes Are Classified
paragraphs: <s>A tropical storm</s> <v>has</v> <o>sustained winds of 62–119 kilometers per hour (39–74 mph)</o>. When a tropical storm has sustained winds greater than 119 kilometers per hour (74 mph) <s>it</s> <v>is classified</v> as <o>a hurricane</o>.

<s>Meteorologists</s> <v>classify</v> <o>hurricanes according to their intensity</o> using <o>the Saffir-Simpson Hurricane Scale</o>. <s>The scale</s> <v>goes</v> from <o>1 to 5</o> and <v>helps describe</v> <o>the kind of damage and dangers of each category of hurricane</o>.
<s>Category One hurricanes</s> <v>have</v> <o>winds of 119–153 kilometers per hour (74–95 mph)</o>. <s>They</s> <v>cause</v> <o>little damage to buildings and minor damage to vegetation</o>.

<s>Category Two hurricanes</s> <v>have</v> <o>winds of 154–177 kilometers per hour (96–110 mph)</o>. <s>They</s> <v>tear away</v> <o>roofing material</o>, <v>damage</v> <o>doors and windows of buildings</o>, and <v>uproot</v> <o>plants</o>. Boats in unprotected areas often break away from docks.

<s>Category Three hurricanes</s> <v>have</v> <o>winds of 178–209 kilometers per hour (111–130 mph)</o>. <s>They</s> <v>cause</v> <o>structural damage to small buildings</o> and <v>destroy</v> <o>mobile homes</o>. Areas lower than 1.52 meters (5 ft.) above sea level may be flooded as far as 13 kilometers (8 mi.) or more inland.

<s>Category Four hurricanes</s> <v>have</v> <o>winds of 211–249 kilometers per hour (131–155 mph)</o>. <s>They</s> <v>cause</v> <o>building walls to collapse and major erosion of beaches</o>. Areas lower than 3 meters (10 ft.) above sea level may be flooded, requiring massive evacuation of residential areas as far as 9 kilometers (6 mi.) inland.

<s>Category Five hurricanes</s> <v>have</v> <o>winds of greater than 249 kilometers per hour (155 mph)</o>. <s>The strongest hurricanes</s> <v>cause</v> <o>extensive damage to houses and industrial buildings</o>. Massive evacuation of residential areas on low ground within 8–16 kilometers (5–10 mi.) of the shoreline may be required.

sections.title: How We Study Hurricanes
paragraphs: <s>We</s> <v>use</v> <o>satellites to locate and track tropical depressions, tropical storms, and hurricanes</o> while they are far out at sea. <s>Satellites orbiting high above Earth</s> <v>take</v> <o>frequent pictures of a hurricane</o>. <s>Scientists</s> <v>study</v> <o>the differences in the pictures</o> to measure changes in a storm's movement and intensity. High clouds within strong hurricanes show up on pictures from satellites as colder than the surrounding area.

<s>The most dramatic hurricane study tool</s> <v>is</v> <o>the "Hurricane Hunter"</o>. This is a specially designed aircraft that flies into the eye of a hurricane to make direct measurements of temperature, moisture, wind speed, and direction.

Doppler radar also <v>helps track</v> <o>storms</o>. <s>Doppler radar stations</s> <v>send out</v> <o>short bursts of energy waves</o>. <s>Rain and clouds</s> <v>reflect</v> <o>these waves back to the station</o>. <s>A computer</s> <v>analyzes</v> <o>the differences in the waves</o> to create a model of a storm. <s>Scientists</s> <v>use</v> <o>the model</o> to show the speed and direction of a storm.

sections.title: How We Predict Hurricanes
paragraphs: <s>Computers</s> <v>are used</v> <o>to predict hurricane movements</o>. <s>Thousands of measurements</s> <v>are typed</v> into <o>supercomputers</o> to predict where a storm will go. As computers improve, <s>we</s> <v>will be able to</v> more accurately <v>predict</v> <o>where hurricanes will hit land</o>. <s>This</s> <v>will save</v> <o>lives and reduce unnecessary evacuations</o>.
But <s>computers</s> <v>will never be</v> <o>100 percent accurate</o> because <s>weather systems</s> <v>are</v> <o>chaotic</o> and <v>are</v> often <o>difficult to predict</o>.

Some researchers believe <s>it</s> <v>is possible</v> <o>to stop hurricanes from forming or to redirect them</o>. One possible way to do this is by seeding clouds in broad areas where hurricanes are born. During seeding, <s>airplanes</s> <v>spray</v> <o>a fine mist of salt powder</o>, which <v>helps</v> <o>water condense out of the air</o>. This would create many small clouds that would use up the energy that might be tapped by a single large storm to become a hurricane.

<s>The problem</s> <v>is</v> that in order to be effective, <s>clouds</s> <v>would have to be seeded</v> over <o>areas the size of Texas</o>. No one yet has come up with a plan that would prevent a hurricane from forming.

sections.title: How to Prepare for Hurricanes
paragraphs: These steps can help protect lives and property during a hurricane:
• Have a family evacuation plan. Include in the plan when to leave, where to go, how you will get there, and what to do if your family becomes separated.
• Keep emergency supplies such as a first aid kit, flashlight, drinking water, and canned food.
• Keep a battery-powered radio to listen to if the power goes out. Follow the instructions you receive over the radio with each elevated warning.
• Keep your car's gas tank full in case you need to evacuate.
• If you are in the path of a hurricane and you have time, protect your house by nailing plywood over windows. Tie down loose objects outside.
• Leave as soon as you are ordered to evacuate.

sections.title: Conclusion
paragraphs: <s>Hurricanes</s> <v>are</v> <o>an awesome display of nature's power</o>. <s>Satellites</s> <v>can tell us</v> <o>much about these storms</o>, such as the direction a hurricane is moving and what areas need to be evacuated.

<s>We</s> <v>may be able to predict</v> <o>the path of hurricanes</o>, but <s>it</s> <v>is unlikely</v> that <s>we</s> <v>will ever be able to stop them</v>. <s>We</s> <v>can protect ourselves</v> by <o>planning where and how we build our homes, by keeping emergency supplies, and by being prepared to evacuate if necessary</o>.

vocabulary.definition: to move people out of danger to a safer area
vocabulary.sentence: Some people farther down the coast evacuate, or leave their homes to go to safer areas.

options: store food and water inside your house for a storm
board up windows with sheets of plywood
listen to weather updates on the radio
move people out of danger to a safer area

vocabulary.definition: the calm center of a hurricane
vocabulary.sentence: The center of a hurricane is called the eye.

options: the outer edge where winds first begin to blow
the calm, often sunny center of the storm
the part of the storm that causes the most rainfall
the area where the storm surge reaches the coast

vocabulary.definition: the wall of clouds and rain around the eye of a hurricane where winds are strongest
vocabulary.sentence: Winds become more and more intense from the edge of the storm to the eyewall.

options: the ring of clouds around the eye where winds are most intense
the calm center where it is sunny and peaceful
the outer boundary of the storm far from the center
the flat top of the hurricane visible from satellites

vocabulary.definition: the degree of force or strength
vocabulary.sentence: Meteorologists classify hurricanes according to their intensity using the Saffir-Simpson Hurricane Scale.

options: the speed at which a hurricane travels across the ocean
the number of hurricanes that form in one season
the degree of force or strength of a storm
the size of the area a hurricane covers

vocabulary.definition: a low-pressure weather system with sustained cyclonic winds of less than 62 km/h (39 mph)
vocabulary.sentence: The process creates a low-pressure area called a tropical depression.

options: a low-pressure area with winds under 62 km/h that may grow into a storm
the most powerful type of hurricane on the Saffir-Simpson Scale
a calm area in the center of a hurricane where there is no wind
a type of rainfall pattern that occurs only in tropical regions

vocabulary.definition: a storm with sustained cyclonic winds of 62–119 km/h (39–74 mph)
vocabulary.sentence: When wind reaches about 62 kilometers per hour, the tropical depression becomes a tropical storm.

quiz.prompt: A tropical storm has sustained winds of:
options: less than 39 miles per hour
greater than 155 miles per hour
exactly 200 kilometers per hour
between 39 and 74 miles per hour

vocabulary.definition: the pressure of air in the atmosphere
vocabulary.sentence: The combination of high winds and low air pressure raises the level of the sea.

options: the force that wind exerts on buildings and trees
the pressure of air in the atmosphere
the weight of ocean water pressing against the shore
the temperature difference between warm and cold air masses

vocabulary.sentence: But computers will never be 100 percent accurate because weather systems are chaotic.

options: perfectly organized and easy to predict
dangerous and likely to cause destruction
random, without an orderly pattern
slow-moving and difficult to track on radar

vocabulary.definition: changes from gas to liquid, forming clouds
vocabulary.sentence: As the warm, moist air rises, it cools and condenses, a process that creates clouds.

options: evaporates into invisible gas high in the atmosphere
freezes into solid ice crystals immediately
changes from gas to liquid, forming clouds
disappears completely into the dry air above

vocabulary.sentence: As hurricanes move over cooler water and over land, they lose much of their energy. The wind slows down, and the storms eventually disperse.

options: grows stronger and more dangerous over warm water
moves directly toward the nearest coastline
breaks up and scatters, losing its organized form
suddenly changes direction and doubles back

vocabulary.definition: a quick rise of the ocean's level caused by strong winds and low air pressure during a hurricane
vocabulary.sentence: Scientists call this a storm surge. It extends for miles around the center of a hurricane and floods any low, coastal land.

options: a quick rise of the ocean's level caused by hurricane winds and low pressure
the heavy rainfall that occurs inside the eye of a hurricane
a sudden drop in air temperature before the storm arrives
the strong winds that blow in the direction opposite to the storm

vocabulary.sentence: A tropical storm has sustained winds of 62–119 kilometers per hour.

options: blow in short, quick bursts and then stop
keep up without stopping for a period of time
change direction constantly as the storm moves
only blow at night when temperatures drop

questions.question: How are typhoons, hurricanes, and severe tropical cyclones alike?
questions.evidence: Hurricanes are gigantic, circular storms that can be hundreds of kilometers wide. When they occur in the Pacific Ocean, they are called typhoons. In the Indian Ocean, they are called severe tropical cyclones.
choices: They all start over land and move toward the ocean.
They all occur only in the Atlantic Ocean near Florida.
They all are gigantic, circular storms with powerful winds.
They all start in the north and move toward the south.
questions.explanation: All three are the same type of storm — gigantic, circular storms. They just have different names depending on which ocean they form in.

questions.question: Where is the wind the strongest in a hurricane?
questions.evidence: Winds become more and more intense from the edge of the storm to the eyewall.
choices: next to the eyewall, where winds rush toward the center
inside the eye, where the air is calm and sunny
far out in front of the storm before it reaches land
at the very top of the clouds above the hurricane
questions.explanation: The eyewall is where winds rushing toward the center suddenly turn upward — this is where winds are the most intense.

questions.question: Which of the following states is least likely to experience a hurricane?
questions.evidence: Central America, islands in the Caribbean, and the East Coast of the United States are hit by hurricanes almost every year. Rarely do hurricanes hit the U.S. West Coast.
choices: Texas, which borders the Gulf of Mexico
Florida, which sticks out into the Atlantic Ocean
Idaho, which is far inland in the northwestern United States
North Carolina, which is on the East Coast
questions.explanation: Idaho is far inland, away from any ocean coast. Hurricanes hit coastal areas — especially the East Coast, Gulf Coast, and Caribbean.

questions.question: When a hurricane is forming, what happens after giant thunderclouds form?
questions.evidence: Soon giant thunderclouds form. Wind begins to rotate, or spin, around the low-pressure area.
choices: The wind begins spinning around the low-pressure area.
A tropical depression forms from the cold, dry air above the ocean.
The storm immediately becomes a hurricane with winds over 74 mph.
The cool air from the ocean drifts above the warm, moist air.
questions.explanation: After thunderclouds form, the next step is that wind begins to rotate (spin) around the low-pressure area. This is a sequence of events in hurricane formation.

questions.question: What is the main idea of the section "How We Study Hurricanes"?
questions.evidence: We use satellites to locate and track tropical depressions, tropical storms, and hurricanes... The most dramatic hurricane study tool is the "Hurricane Hunter." ... Doppler radar also helps track storms.
choices: Satellites can track storms from a great distance away.
The "Hurricane Hunter" aircraft is designed to fly into the eye.
Doppler radar stations send out short bursts of energy waves.
Scientists use multiple advanced tools — satellites, aircraft, and radar — to study hurricanes.
questions.explanation: The section discusses satellites, Hurricane Hunter aircraft, AND Doppler radar — all different tools scientists use. The main idea covers all of them, not just one.

questions.question: Why do hurricanes usually form in the summers of the Northern and Southern Hemispheres?
questions.evidence: The storms form when energy from the Sun heats ocean water near the equator. Warm, moist air evaporating from the ocean is lighter than the cold, dry air above it.
choices: The Sun's energy heats ocean water near the equator, creating the warm air that fuels storms.
High-pressure systems are created more often in summer months.
Sea level rises in summer, allowing storms to form more easily.
Cool air reaches Earth's surface more often during the summer.
questions.explanation: In summer, the Sun heats ocean water near the equator. This warm water provides the energy hurricanes need to form.

questions.question: Why might people think the storm is over when they are in the eye of a hurricane?
questions.evidence: Yet, inside the eye, it's often calm and sunny. Most hurricane eyes are 30–60 kilometers (20–40 mi.) in diameter.
choices: Because weather stations often announce that the storm has ended.
Because the hurricane is weakening as it reaches the eye.
Because the weather inside the eye is calm and sunny.
Because the wind direction suddenly reverses in the eye.
questions.explanation: Inside the eye, it's calm and sunny — so people might think the storm has passed. But the other side of the storm will hit soon!

questions.question: Which of the following can cause a tropical storm to become a hurricane?
questions.evidence: Tropical storms can grow into hurricanes if they soak up more heat energy from the warm oceans they pass over.
choices: The tropical storm passes over land and gains strength from friction.
The tropical storm soaks up more heat energy as it passes over warm oceans.
The tropical storm combines with other tropical depressions nearby.
The tropical storm gains energy from cool layers of the atmosphere.
questions.explanation: Tropical storms need warm ocean water for fuel. When they soak up more heat energy from warm oceans, they can grow into hurricanes.

questions.question: What characteristic of hurricanes causes them to lose strength?
questions.evidence: As hurricanes move over cooler water and over land, they lose much of their energy. The wind slows down, and the storms eventually disperse.
choices: They stay over warm ocean water for too long.
They move away from the equator and warm ocean water that fuels them.
They form bands of clouds that spread out from the center.
They rotate faster as they move farther from where they formed.
questions.explanation: Hurricanes lose strength when they move away from warm ocean water (their energy source). Moving over cooler water or land cuts off their fuel supply.

questions.question: What causes a storm surge during a hurricane?
questions.evidence: As hurricanes approach shore, the combination of high winds and low air pressure actually raises the level of the sea around the storm higher than normal. Scientists call this a storm surge.
choices: Heavy rainfall from the eyewall floods coastal roads and beaches.
The combination of high winds and low air pressure raises the sea level around the storm.
Underwater earthquakes near the coast push water upward.
The eye of the hurricane creates a vacuum that pulls water up from the ocean floor.
questions.explanation: A storm surge is caused by the combination of high winds and low air pressure — together they push the sea level higher than normal.

questions.question: Explain how computers can help save lives during a hurricane. Why will computer predictions never be 100 percent accurate?
questions.explanation: Computers can predict hurricane paths by processing thousands of measurements, helping people evacuate in time. But weather systems are chaotic and don't follow simple rules, so predictions can never be perfect.
opinionPrompts: How do supercomputers help predict hurricane paths?
Why are weather systems described as "chaotic"?
What evidence from the text supports your answer?

problemSolution.title: Hurricane Frances Approaches Florida
problemSolution.context: A hurricane watch is issued for the Florida east coast. People in a small cafe hear the warning on the radio while it's still sunny outside.

problem: The radio station stopped broadcasting weather reports
A powerful hurricane is heading toward the Florida coast and people need to get ready
People in the cafe could not see the ocean from where they sat
The grocery store ran out of batteries in the winter
cause: The weather was too hot and people wanted to leave
A earthquake warning was issued for the east coast
Hurricane Frances is about 555 miles away and moving toward the coast at 14 mph
The cafe owner told everyone to go home early
effect: Everyone ignored the warning because it was still sunny outside
The hurricane changed direction and hit a different state
The government closed all schools in the United States
People bought plywood to board up windows and stocked up on water, food, and batteries
solution: Some people evacuated to safer areas while others prepared their homes with supplies
They waited until the hurricane arrived to decide what to do
They called the National Hurricane Center to ask it to stop the storm
They moved all their furniture to the roof of their houses

problemSolution.title: Storm Surge Floods the Coast
problemSolution.context: A hurricane is approaching the shore. The combination of high winds and low air pressure causes the sea level to rise dramatically.

problem: The ocean water temperature dropped suddenly near the coast
The combination of high winds and low air pressure raises the sea level, flooding coastal land
Heavy rainfall inside the eye caused rivers to overflow
The hurricane's name was removed from the official list
cause: Fishermen left their boats in unprotected areas near the dock
The sun heated the ocean water near the equator
High winds and low air pressure push the sea level higher than normal
Doppler radar stations sent energy waves into the storm
effect: The eye of the hurricane became calm and sunny for several hours
Category Five winds destroyed all buildings along the shoreline
The hurricane changed from a Category Three to a Category One
Low-lying coastal areas flood, causing major property damage
solution: Scientists seeded the clouds with salt powder to stop the storm
Meteorologists warned residents to evacuate low-lying coastal areas
The government built a wall around the entire coastline
People opened their windows to equalize the air pressure

problemSolution.title: How a Tropical Storm Becomes a Hurricane
problemSolution.context: A tropical depression forms over warm ocean water near the equator. Energy from the Sun has heated the water, creating warm, moist air that rises.

problem: The tropical depression needs more energy to grow stronger
Cold, dry air is sinking down and blocking the warm air
The ocean water near the equator is too cold for storms to form
Satellites cannot track the storm because it is too small
cause: The Sun's energy heats ocean water, creating warm moist air that rises and creates a low-pressure area
Hurricane Hunter aircraft flew into the storm and measured the wind speed
Doppler radar reflected energy waves off the thunderclouds
The Saffir-Simpson Scale was used to classify the storm
effect: The storm immediately became a Category Five hurricane
Giant thunderclouds form, wind begins to rotate, and if wind speeds exceed 119 km/h, it becomes a hurricane
The storm moved over land and lost all its energy
The storm dispersed before reaching tropical storm status
solution: The storm must move over cooler water to gain strength
Computers must predict the storm's path before it forms
The tropical storm must soak up more heat energy from warm oceans to grow into a hurricane
Meteorologists must name the storm before it can become a hurricane

problemSolution.title: Why Can't We Predict Hurricanes Perfectly?
problemSolution.context: Scientists use supercomputers to predict where hurricanes will go, but their predictions are never 100 percent accurate.

problem: The satellites orbiting Earth take pictures too infrequently
The Hurricane Hunter aircraft cannot fly into every storm
Computer predictions of hurricane paths are never completely accurate
Doppler radar stations are too far from the ocean to detect storms
cause: Weather systems are chaotic and do not follow simple, predictable rules
Computers are not powerful enough to process the data
Scientists do not have enough satellites to track all storms
The Saffir-Simpson Scale has too many categories to predict
effect: People may evacuate unnecessarily or not leave when they should
All hurricane predictions are completely wrong every time
The National Hurricane Center stopped issuing warnings
Scientists switched back to predicting weather without computers
solution: Build more Doppler radar stations along every coastline
As computers improve, predictions become more accurate — but will never be perfect because weather is chaotic
Stop trying to predict hurricanes and just wait for them to arrive
Seed all clouds with salt powder before they can form hurricanes

intro.title: Evacuate
intro.content: Means to leave a dangerous area and go somewhere safe. When a hurricane is coming, people evacuate their homes!

intro.title: Adverb: Rapidly
intro.content: "The wind speed picks up" — notice how the text uses adverbs to describe HOW things happen. Can you find adverbs in this section?

intro.title: Cause & Effect
intro.content: The hurricane watch (cause) → people buy plywood and supplies (effect). Look for more cause-and-effect pairs as you read!

whatIs.title: Eye vs. Eyewall
whatIs.content: The EYE is the calm center — sunny and peaceful. The EYEWALL surrounds it — this is where winds are STRONGEST. Don't mix them up!

whatIs.title: Storm Surge
whatIs.content: When hurricane winds + low air pressure PUSH the sea level higher = storm surge. This causes MORE damage than wind alone!

whatIs.title: Compare & Contrast
whatIs.content: Hurricane = Atlantic Ocean. Typhoon = Pacific Ocean. Severe tropical cyclone = Indian Ocean. Same storm, different names!

formation.title: Condenses
formation.content: When warm air rises and cools, water vapor condenses = changes from gas back to liquid, forming clouds. This releases HEAT energy!

formation.title: Adverb: Rapidly
formation.content: "The upward-moving air rapidly cools" — 'rapidly' is an adverb describing HOW the air cools. It comes from the adjective 'rapid.'

formation.title: Formation Steps
formation.content: 1) Sun heats ocean → 2) Warm air rises → 3) Low pressure (tropical depression) → 4) Thunderclouds → 5) Wind rotates (tropical storm) → 6) Winds > 119 km/h = HURRICANE!

where.title: Caribbean
where.content: The Caribbean is a region in the Atlantic Ocean between North and South America. It gets hit by hurricanes almost every year!

where.title: Why Not the West Coast?
where.content: The text says 'Rarely do hurricanes hit the U.S. West Coast.' Why? Ocean currents and wind patterns push storms away from it!

classified.title: Intensity
classified.content: How strong or powerful something is. The Saffir-Simpson Scale measures hurricane intensity from 1 (weakest) to 5 (strongest).

classified.title: Sustained
classified.content: Kept up without stopping. 'Sustained winds' means winds that keep blowing at that speed — not just a quick gust!

classified.title: Numbers & Abbreviations
classified.content: This book uses both metric (km/h) and imperial (mph). 'mph' = miles per hour. When reading '0.78 in.' say 'zero point seven eight inches.'

study.title: Hurricane Hunter
study.content: A specially designed aircraft that flies INTO the eye of a hurricane! It measures temperature, moisture, wind speed, and direction directly.

study.title: Three Tools
study.content: Scientists use 3 tools: 1) SATELLITES (track from space), 2) HURRICANE HUNTER (fly in for direct measurements), 3) DOPPLER RADAR (track wind speed from ground).

predict.title: Chaotic
predict.content: Random, without an orderly pattern. Weather is chaotic — that's why computers can NEVER be 100% accurate at predicting hurricanes!

predict.title: Cause & Effect
predict.content: Cause: Weather systems are chaotic. Effect: Computer predictions will never be 100% accurate. This is a key cause-and-effect relationship!

prepare.title: Preparation Steps
prepare.content: Key preparations: 1) Family evacuation plan, 2) Emergency supplies, 3) Battery-powered radio, 4) Full gas tank, 5) Plywood on windows, 6) Leave when ordered!

conclusion.title: Main Idea
conclusion.content: Hurricanes are nature's awesome power. We can predict their path (but not perfectly) and we can't stop them — so we must PREPARE!
